query: efficacy of deucravacitinib in treatment of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib is a targeted treatment for patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis who qualify for systemic therapy or phototherapy. This medication selectively inhibits the tyrosine kinase 2 (Tyk2) protein, providing greater efficacy and a superior safety profile compared to other biological agents, particularly apremilast. Deucravacitinib may be administered orally once daily, which enhances patient compliance and improves treatment outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Following an oral dose of deucravacitinib, the Cmax and AUC increased proportionally, exhibiting linear pharmacokinetics. The absolute bioavailability of deucravacitinib was 99%, and the median Tmax was achieved in 2 to 3 hours. [22] The PK of deucravacitinib and its major active metabolite (BMT-153261) were almost comparable between healthy controls and patients with psoriasis. [3] A once-daily 6 mg dosing regimen resulted in a steady-state Cmax and AUC of deucravacitinib of 45 ng/mL and 473 ng/mL, respectively. The same dose resulted in a steady-state Cmax and AUC 24 of BMT-153261 of 5 ng/mL and 95 ng·hr/mL, respectively. [3] No significant differences were observed after a calorie-dense, high-fat meal of 51 total kcal with an approximate macronutrient distribution of 33% carbohydrates, 15% protein, and 52% fat. [3] When administered with food, the Cmax and AUC of deucravacitinib were reduced by approximately 24% and 11%, respectively, and the Tmax was prolonged by 1 hour. When administered with food, Cmax and AUC of BMT-153261 were reduced by approximately 23% and 10%, respectively, and the Tmax was prolonged by 2 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: In one clinical trial, 1519 patients with plaque psoriasis received deucravacitinib therapy. Of these, 152 patients (10%) were 65 or older, and 21 patients (1.4%) were 75. During the first 16 weeks of observation, severe adverse reactions occurred in 80 patients older than 65 and 12 patients older than 75; these patients were administered deucravacitinib without switching treatment arms. This rate of severe reactions was determined to be significantly higher than in younger patients. These severe effects included infection and many required treatment discontinuation. No significant difference in efficacy was observed between older and younger adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib has consistently demonstrated a safety profile. [3] The safety of deucravacitinib was assessed in 2 placebo-controlled and active-controlled (PSO-1 and PSO-2) clinical trials and an open-label-extension (OLE) trial in which patients could enroll after completing the PSO-1 or PSO-2 trials. A total of 1519 patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis received deucravacitinib 6 mg once daily. Of these, 1141 patients were exposed to deucravacitinib for at least 12 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: At week 16 in the PSO-1 trial, significantly higher response rates were observed in patients receiving deucravacitinib compared to placebo or apremilast. Therapeutic efficacy improved with treatment longer than 16 weeks and was maintained through week 52. At week 16 in the PSO-2 trial, more than 50% of patients receiving deucravacitinib achieved superior clearing compared to apremilast. Across multiple efficacy endpoints, deucravacitinib consistently demonstrated superiority to apremilast and was well tolerated in patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis. [25] A large proportion of patients treated with deucravacitinib reported a Psoriasis Symptoms and Signs Diary (PSSD) symptoms score of 0 at week 16, indicating relief from itching, pain, stinging, burning, and skin tightness (8% in deucravacitinib group versus 1% in the placebo arm) in both clinical trials. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The patients are counseled to immediately seek professional assistance upon experiencing adverse reactions. Patients with plaque psoriasis often experience major depression with a sense of lost control over their medical condition and routine lives. The interprofessional healthcare team plays a crucial role in empowering the patients by promoting a perception of control and demonstrating comprehensive and collaborative efforts. This approach facilitates positive healthcare outcomes in the patients receiving deucravacitinib therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized pustular psoriasis [3] [1] Erythrodermic psoriasis [3] [1] Psoriatic arthritis [6] [7] [8] Moderate-to-severe scalp psoriasis [9] Alopecia areata and alopecia universalis [10] Nail psoriasis [11] [12] Oral psoriasis [13] Plaque psoriasis involving the palms and soles [12] Systemic lupus erythematosus [7] [14] Discoid lupus erythematosus [15] Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus [5] Lupus nephritis Inflammatory bowel disease (ie, Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis) [7] [16] Atopic dermatitis [16] Erosive oral lichen planus [17] Rheumatoid arthritis [16] [18] Dermatomyositis [18] Spondyloarthropathies (eg, ankylosing spondylitis) [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disorder. The disease burden is equally distributed in both genders worldwide. This condition causes physical disfigurement, which has a profound psychosocial impact, leading to low self-esteem, stigmatization, and isolation. Additionally, it reduces the lifespan of patients due to associated comorbidities. The expensive and prolonged treatment imposes a substantial economic burden on the patient and healthcare system. The community needs safer and more effective treatment to address these issues. Deucravacitinib is a breakthrough in the targeted treatment of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis. [3] The interdisciplinary healthcare team must work together to ensure the therapeutic safety and efficacy of deucravacitinib.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib is the first Janus kinase (Jak) inhibitor medication found to inhibit tyrosine kinase 2 (Tyk2) selectively. [1] [2] The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved deucravacitinib on September 9th, 2022, as a treatment for adult patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis, provided these patients are candidates for systemic therapy or phototherapy. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibition of Tyk2 promotes downregulation of the IL-23 (T H 17), type I interferon, IL-12, and keratinocyte activation pathways. However, the precise mechanism linking Tyk2 inhibition to the effective treatment of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis is unknown. [3] In patients with psoriasis, deucravacitinib reduces the expression of psoriasis-associated genes in a dose-dependent fashion. This includes reducing type I IFN pathway-regulated genes and the IL-23 pathway. Deucravacitinib reduces IL-17A levels by 47% to 50%, IL-19 by 72%, and β-defensin by 81% to 84% after once-daily administration for 16 weeks. However, the mechanism linking these reduced inflammation marker levels to the clinical effects of deucravacitinib has not been established.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients on deucravacitinib require follow-up visits to evaluate their response to therapy. Close monitoring of the patients for prompt detection and effective management of adverse drug reactions such as infections, rhabdomyolysis, hepatic dysfunction, and hypertriglyceridemia is required. [24] In addition, patients are assessed for complications associated with psoriasis. At a dosage range up to 6 times the recommended daily dosage of 6 mg, deucravacitinib does not cause any clinically significant prolongation of QTc interval. [29] Patients receiving deucravacitinib therapy should be monitored using the following markers: Triglycerides Lipids Liver enzymes Creatine phosphokinase Glomerular filtration rate

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Various diseases involve the overexpression of type I interferons (IFN-α and β), including systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), systemic sclerosis, and Sjögren syndrome. Patients with these conditions may benefit from deucravacitinib therapy. [5] Multiple studies have demonstrated the beneficial effects of deucravacitinib therapy for patients with various autoimmune conditions other than plaque psoriasis. Despite this demonstrated benefit, the FDA has not approved the administration of deucravacitinib for these patients. These off-label uses include:

retrieved_docs.title: Plaque Psoriasis -- Treatment / Management -- Salicylic Acid
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin is effective for keratoderma and pustular psoriasis phenotypes; however, its teratogenicity necessitates strict avoidance of pregnancy for up to three years following treatment. Oral alternatives include targeted small molecules such as apremilast—a PDE4 inhibitor—and JAK inhibitors, such as deucravacitinib and tofacitinib. Apremilast offers a favorable safety profile, primarily limited to gastrointestinal symptoms and weight loss, and requires minimal laboratory monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib is available as a 6 mg oral tablet.

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Brodalumab is a monoclonal antibody approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of moderate to severe plaque psoriasis in adult patients who have failed treatment with topical and other systemic therapy. [1] [2] [3] Results from phase 3 randomized, double-blinded studies have demonstrated that patients treated with brodalumab can achieve significant clinical improvement compared to those treated with placebo. By week 12 on brodalumab, 83% of patients achieved a 75% reduction in psoriasis area and severity score (PASI 75) compared to 3% of patients on placebo, and almost 42% were able to achieve a PASI 100 response rate. Similarly, 76% of patients achieved a static Physician’s Global Assessment sPGA success (defined as an sPGA score of 0 or 1).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin is a member of the class of retinoids that are used for the treatment of psoriasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2585
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPSORIATICS constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to treat psoriasis, and antipsoriatics for systemic use are a specific subset of this class that are administered orally, intravenously, or by other routes to exert effects throughout the body rather than locally on the skin. This relationship indicates that systemic antipsoriatic agents are encompassed within the broader category of antipsoriatics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
retrieved_docs.content: Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcitriol is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a member of the group designated “Other antipsoriatics for topical use,” indicating that it is employed as a topical medication for the treatment of psoriasis and is grouped with other agents in this therapeutic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason, a potent glucocorticoid, is commonly employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), where it helps to diminish inflammation and improve the characteristic skin lesions of the disease. This therapeutic link highlights the important role of corticosteroid agents such as betamethason in treating inflammatory dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2035
retrieved_docs.content: Antracen derivatives, topical antipsoriatics and psoralens for topical use are both classified as antipsoriatics for topical use, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of topical antipsoriatic agents employed in the treatment of psoriasis. This shared classification highlights that both Antracen‑derived compounds and psoralen preparations are utilized as skin‑applied therapies aimed at reducing psoriatic lesions. Recognizing these agents as members of the same therapeutic class assists clinicians in selecting appropriate topical treatments for patients with psoriasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin, a retinoid used for the systemic treatment of psoriasis, is a member of the group “Retinoids for treatment of psoriasis,” which in turn is classified as an “antipsoriatics for systemic use” and therefore falls under the broader category of ANTIPSORIATICS. While these agents are valuable for managing severe psoriasis, acitretin is contraindicated in patients with liver disorder (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد). The non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug nabumeton (nabumetone) shares the same contraindication for liver disorder. Additionally, other systemic drugs—including the androgenic agent danazol, the progestin hydroxyprogesteron kaproát (hydroxyprogesterone caproate), and the beta‑blocker nebivolol hydrochlorid (nebivolol hydrochloride)—are also contraindicated in the presence of liver disorder. Awareness of these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care when using antipsoriatics, especially those administered systemically.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 152
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin, hydroxyprogesterone caproate, nebivolol, nebivolol hydrochloride, ticlopidine, clavulanate and nabumetone are contraindicated in patients with liver disorder, underscoring the need for hepatic function assessment before prescribing these agents. Nebivolol is also a component of the fixed‑dose combination nebivolol/valsartan; this product belongs to the broader class of angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) combinations, which may be formulated with calcium‑channel blockers and are classified as members of the nebivolol/valsartan group. Aztreonam is a monobactam, a structural class that shares a β‑lactam core with cefmetazole, cilastatin, clavulanate, loracarbef and tazobactam. In the therapeutic hierarchy of psoriasis, antipsoriatics for systemic use constitute a subset of antipsoriatics, and within this subset the class “retinoids for treatment of psoriasis” is defined; acitretin is a member of this retinoid class, linking it to systemic antipsoriatic therapy. Torasemide

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 328
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin is a retinoid that is used systemically for the treatment of psoriasis and is classified as a member of the “retinoids for treatment of psoriasis” group, which in turn is a subtype of antipsoriatics for systemic use. Because acitretin belongs to this systemic antipsoriatic class, it shares the therapeutic intent of other systemic antipsoriatic agents but is specifically contraindicated in patients with liver disorder (اضطراب الكَبِد). Similarly, nebivolol hydrochloride, ticlopidine, and nabumetone are also contraindicated in the presence of liver disorder, highlighting the importance of hepatic function assessment before prescribing these drugs. Alglucerase is an enzyme‑replacement/modifier medication that belongs to the therapeutic class “Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC]”. It is indicated for the treatment of liver disorder, indicating that enzyme‑replacement therapy can be employed when hepatic disease is present. Hyaluronoglukosaminidasa is another agent within the same “Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC]” class, underscoring a broader category of enzymatic therapies used for various metabolic or organ‑specific conditions. Overall, the knowledge base links systemic antipsoriatic agents such as acitretin with liver safety concerns, while also grouping enzyme‑replacement drugs like alglucerase and hyaluronoglukosaminidasa under a common therapeutic class that can address liver‑related pathology. Recognizing these contraindications and class relationships is essential for safe prescribing and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and fluocinonid are classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroids, a high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid agents. This classification indicates that both amcinonide and fluocinonid share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, including strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that are commonly utilized in dermatologic therapy for inflammatory skin conditions. Recognizing amcinonide and fluocinonid as members of this potent corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate potency levels, balance therapeutic efficacy with safety, and guide appropriate short‑term use to limit systemic exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1328
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPSORIATICS represent a broad class of medications used to treat psoriasis, and within this class there exists a specific subgroup designated as antipsoriatics for systemic use. Retinoids for treatment of psoriasis are classified as a type of antipsoriatics for systemic use, indicating that these retinoid agents are employed systemically to manage psoriasis symptoms. This hierarchical relationship highlights that systemic antipsoriatic therapy is a subset of the overall antipsoriatic category, with retinoid drugs being a particular example of systemic treatment options for psoriasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: structures and derived from amide precursors. The intravenous amino‑acid additive formulation contains alanylglutamine (derived from the dipeptide alanylglutamine) and arginine hydrochloride, the latter also appearing as an inactive ingredient in a capsaicin 0.5 mg/mL topical cream and potentially enhancing therapeutic effect by increasing blood and tissue alkalinity. The additive also includes antifibrinolytic amino acids (antifibrinolytika). Systemic antipsoriatics are represented by the retinoid acitretin, which belongs to the “Retinoids for treatment of psoriasis” group, and by the brand‑name Abicol tablet that is associated with Rauwolfia alkaloids. Together, these interrelationships clarify structural similarities, formulation hierarchies, therapeutic categories, mechanistic actions, and contraindications that are essential for informed clinical decision‑making and safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.
